criteria_results.title: Allegation 1: Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies cherry-picking as a distinct allegation category. Under the 'Cherry-picking Scheme' section, the output explicitly describes the pattern of disproportionately allocating profitable trades to Oceanic Fund I LP (68.4% profitable allocation rate vs. 41.2% for other accounts) and to Chao's personal account (81.6% profitable allocation rate). The output quantifies the excess profits ($7,800,000 to Oceanic Fund I LP and $438,000 to Chao's personal account, totaling $8,238,000), identifies the responsible parties (Whitmore, Chao), and cites the applicable statutory provisions (Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act). The cherry-picking allegation is treated as 'Allegation Category 1' throughout the document.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Correct Statutory Provisions Cited
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly cites both Section 206(1) and Section 206(2) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 in connection with the cherry-picking allegation. For example, the output states: 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking," in violation of Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act.' Additionally, the output notes that 'Section 206(1) of the Advisers Act [15 U.S.C. § 80b-6(1)] --- Prohibits any investment adviser from employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client. The Division alleges that this provision was violated by the conduct described in Allegation Category 1 (Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations)...' These citations clearly satisfy the criterion.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: WCA Identified as Implicated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies WCA (Whitmore Capital Advisors LLC) as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation. The output states: 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking," in violation of Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act.' Additionally, the output notes 'Whitmore Capital Advisors LLC --- WCA is implicated in all six allegation categories described in this notice' and 'The Division alleges a pattern of "cherry-picking" (favorable trade allocation) by WCA, Whitmore, and Chao during the Review Period.' WCA is clearly and repeatedly identified as an implicated party in the cherry-picking allegation.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Whitmore Identified as Implicated (12% LP Interest)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies Whitmore as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation due to his 12% LP interest in Oceanic Fund I LP. Multiple sections state that 'Whitmore holds a 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP valued at approximately $22.2 million as of December 31, 2023' and that 'The Division alleges that the favorable allocation to Oceanic Fund I LP was motivated by Whitmore's personal financial interest in that vehicle.' The output also notes Whitmore is 'implicated in five of six allegation categories' including cherry-picking, and describes his dual financial incentives (LP interest appreciation plus indirect GP compensation) as establishing motive for the alleged scheme.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Chao Identified as Implicated (Personal Account)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies Diana L. Chao as implicated in the cherry-picking allegation due to her personal account receiving favorable allocations. Multiple sections explicitly state that Chao's personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) received 87 allocations from omnibus fills, of which 71 (81.6%) were same-day profitable, substantially higher than the 41.2% rate for other accounts. The output also states that Chao 'personally benefited from the cherry-picking pattern by receiving an outsized share of profitable trade allocations in her personal account' and that she is implicated in 'Allegation Category 1 (Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations, as the Head of Trading who was directly responsible for allocation decisions and who personally benefited from the cherry-picking pattern through favorable allocations to her personal account).'

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Review Period January 1, 2021 â€“ December 31, 2023
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the Review Period as 'January 1, 2021 -- December 31, 2023' multiple times throughout the document, including in the context of the cherry-picking scheme. The cherry-picking allegations are clearly tied to this time period throughout the output.

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies misleading Form ADV Part 2A disclosures as a distinct allegation category. criteria_results.title: Form ADV: False Pro Rata Allocation Statement in Item 6 Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the false statement in Item 6 of WCA's Form ADV Part 2A. (Account No. ending -7823) that received allocations from the Firm's omnibus trading account.' Both omissions are identified multiple times throughout the output with supporting analysis.

criteria_results.title: Allegation 3: Deficient Compliance Policies and Procedures â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies deficient compliance policies and procedures as a distinct allegation category. The output references 'Allegation Category 3' in multiple places (e.g., 'Allegation Category 3' in the context of Chao's personal trading), and extensively discusses the compliance manual gaps under Rule 206(4)-7, including: Section 7.3's failure to address omnibus trade allocation procedures, Section 9.1's failure to restrict employees from receiving omnibus allocations in personal accounts, and the Hargrove & Tilton annual review's failure to test trade allocation patterns. The output also includes a numbered allegation item (7) stating 'The Division alleges that WCA's written compliance policies and procedures...failed to comply with the requirements of Rule 206(4)-7 under the Advisers Act.' This clearly identifies deficient compliance policies and procedures as a distinct allegation category.

criteria_results.title: Deficient Compliance Policies: Rule 206(4)-7 Referenced
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly and repeatedly cites Rule 206(4)-7 under the Investment Advisers Act in connection with the deficient compliance policies allegation. For example, it states: 'Rule 206(4)-7 requires each investment adviser registered with the Commission to adopt and implement written policies and procedures reasonably designed to prevent violation of the Advisers Act' and 'The Division alleges that WCA's written compliance policies and procedures, as set forth in the Firm's Compliance Manual (last updated November 15, 2022), failed to comply with the requirements of Rule 206(4)-7 under the Advisers Act.' The rule is cited multiple times throughout the document in direct connection with the deficient compliance policies allegation.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Section 7.3 Deficiency Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the Section 7.3 deficiency multiple times by section number. For example, it states: 'Section 7.3 of the Compliance Manual, titled "Trade Allocation," described a "rotation" methodology for the allocation of IPO (initial public offering) and secondary offering allocations among eligible client accounts. However, Section 7.3 contained no procedures whatsoever for the allocation of equity trades executed through omnibus accounts.' This directly satisfies the criterion by identifying that Section 7.3 addressed only IPO/secondary allocation rotation but had no procedures for omnibus equity trade allocation.

criteria_results.title: Compliance Policies: Section 9.1 Deficiency Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the Section 9.1 deficiency multiple times. For example: 'Section 9.1 of the Compliance Manual, titled "Personal Trading," required employees to pre-clear trades in individual securities and to report personal securities transactions on a quarterly basis and personal securities holdings on an annual basis, consistent with the requirements of Rule 204A-1 under the Advisers Act. However, Section 9.1 did not address or restrict employees from receiving allocations from the Firm's omnibus account in their personal brokerage accounts.' This directly satisfies the criterion by identifying Section 9.1 by name and noting that while it required pre-clearance, it failed to address employees receiving omnibus allocations.

criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies failure to maintain required books and records as a distinct allegation category (Allegation Category 4) and cites both Rule 204-2(a)(3) and Rule 204-2(a)(7) under the Advisers Act multiple times throughout the document. The output references '45.2% of omnibus trades (2,614 of 5,776)' lacking written allocation records, post-dated metadata on 312 Excel spreadsheets, and directly quotes the regulatory requirements of Rule 204-2(a)(3) (memoranda of each order including allocation) and Rule 204-2(a)(7) (originals/copies of written communications relating to recommendations and order execution).

criteria_results.title: Books and Records: Key Quantitative Facts
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output includes all three data points: (1) 2,614 of 5,776 omnibus trades (45.2%) lacked any written allocation rationale - mentioned multiple times; (2) 312 Excel spreadsheets had metadata showing creation dates post-dating trade dates - mentioned multiple times; (3) average creation lag of 17 business days - also mentioned multiple times. All three criteria are clearly present, satisfying the requirement of at least 2 of 3.

criteria_results.title: Allegation 5: Bellini Directed Brokerage / Kickback Scheme â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies Bellini's directed brokerage scheme as a distinct allegation category (Allegation Category 5: Breach of Fiduciary Duty / Directed Brokerage Arrangement). It describes that Bellini directed 23 block trades totaling approximately $48.7 million in notional value (approximately 9.4 million shares) to Lakeshore Trading LLC, and that Kevin Ostrowski, a 40% owner of Lakeshore Trading, was Bellini's college roommate at the University of Virginia (1999-2003). These details are repeated multiple times throughout the output with consistent specificity.

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Commission Rate Disparity Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the commission rate disparity multiple times: 'Lakeshore Trading charged WCA's clients commissions averaging 4.2 cents per share on the 23 directed trades. By comparison, WCA's other executing brokers --- including Meridian Clearing Corp. and two other broker-dealers used by the Firm during the Review Period --- charged commissions averaging 1.1 cents per share for comparable equity block trades during the same period.' This appears in multiple sections of the output, clearly satisfying the criterion.

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Excess Commissions Total Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the excess commissions total of approximately $291,400 on approximately 9.4 million shares multiple times throughout the document, including the calculation: '9,400,000 shares × ($0.042 average commission per share paid to Lakeshore Trading − $0.011 average commission per share paid to other executing brokers) = 9,400,000 shares × $0.031 excess commission per share = $291,400 in excess commissions.'

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Undisclosed Compensation from Ostrowski
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies that Bellini received undisclosed compensation from Ostrowski consisting of a $45,000 country club initiation fee and monthly dues of $2,800/month for 17 months totaling $47,600, with a combined total of $92,600. This is stated multiple times throughout the output, including: 'The total undisclosed compensation received by Bellini, in the form of payments made by Ostrowski to Bayshore Palms Golf & Country Club for Bellini's benefit, was approximately $92,600 ($45,000 initiation fee + $47,600 in monthly dues = $92,600).'

criteria_results.title: Bellini Scheme: Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund Identified as Victim
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies the Crescent Bay Municipal Pension Fund as a victim of Bellini's directed brokerage scheme in multiple places. The Division's preliminary analysis indicates that the Crescent Bay Pension Fund bore approximately $187,000 of the $291,400 in excess commissions, representing approximately 64% of the total.' This directly connects Crescent Bay to Bellini's directed brokerage scheme as a harmed client.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_001: Trade Count Discrepancy (4,217 vs. 4,312)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not identify the discrepancy between the 4,217 omnibus equity trades referenced in the referral notice and the 4,312 trades referenced in the statistical exhibit (sec-quantitative-analysis-summary.xlsx). Throughout the output, the agent consistently uses the 4,217 figure without noting any conflict with a 4,312 figure from the quantitative analysis summary. The discrepancies section at the end of the output identifies three other discrepancies (file deletion instruction year, undisclosed compensation total, and Access Person initial holdings report deadline days), but makes no mention of the 4,217 vs. 4,312 trade count discrepancy.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_001: Source Documents Correctly Attributed
criteria_results.reasoning: The criterion requires the output to attribute the 4,217 figure to the referral notice (sec-enforcement-referral-notice.docx) AND the 4,312 figure to the statistical summary (sec-quantitative-analysis-summary.xlsx). The discrepancy between 4,217 and 4,312 is never identified or addressed in the output, so the source document attribution for this specific discrepancy is not present.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_002: Criminal Obstruction Exposure Flagged (18 U.S.C. Â§Â§ 1505/1519)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly flags parallel criminal exposure under 18 U.S.C. § 1519 (destroying, altering, or falsifying records with intent to impede a federal agency investigation, carrying up to 20 years) and 18 U.S.C. § 1505 (corruptly obstructing a pending federal agency proceeding, up to 5 years) in the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section. It also specifically notes that 'SEC Enforcement routinely refers obstruction and spoliation conduct to DOJ under parallel-proceedings practice' and advises counsel to 'assume a criminal referral is possible' and evaluate Fifth Amendment implications. This directly satisfies the criterion requiring the output to flag criminal exposure beyond the civil SEC enforcement action for the obstruction allegation.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_003: Bellini Compensation Calculation Gap ($92,600 vs. $103,800)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output identifies a discrepancy in Bellini's undisclosed compensation, but it flags a $2,600 discrepancy between $45,000 and $47,600 (treating these as alternative totals rather than components). The agent does not identify the specific discrepancy described in the criterion: that the bank records exhibit shows 21 months of payments (May 2022 through January 2024) at $2,800/month = $58,800 in dues plus $45,000 initiation = $103,800 total, versus the referral notice's calculation of 17 months ($47,600) plus $45,000 = $92,600, creating an approximately $11,200 gap. The agent notes the bank records show entries including 'Total Months of Dues: 21' but never connects this to a recalculation of the total compensation figure or identifies the resulting $11,200 discrepancy between what the bank records support and what the referral notice claims.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_003: Defense Significance of Compensation Gap Noted
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output identifies a discrepancy in undisclosed compensation figures ($45,000 vs. $47,600, noting a $2,600 gap), but this is not the same discrepancy referenced in the criterion ($92,600 vs. $103,800). The criterion asks specifically about implications of a $92,600 vs. $103,800 total compensation discrepancy. The agent never identifies or discusses a $103,800 figure anywhere in the output, nor does it discuss implications of that specific gap. The agent does note that the $2,600 discrepancy 'undermines the accuracy of the enforcement referral's quantification of misconduct and could impact damages exposure or credibility,' but this addresses a different discrepancy than what the criterion specifies. Since the criterion requires discussion of the $92,600 vs. $103,800 discrepancy and its implications, and the agent never identifies or analyzes that specific discrepancy, the criterion is not satisfied.

criteria_results.title: Scienter Distinction Between Section 206(1) and 206(2) Flagged
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies the scienter distinction between Section 206(1) and Section 206(2) in the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section. It states: 'Section 206(2) requires only NEGLIGENCE, not scienter — a lower bar than Section 206(1). The same conduct may sustain a 206(2) claim even where intent for 206(1) cannot be established.' This directly flags the scienter vs. negligence distinction as a defense issue, satisfying the criterion. The output also notes this in the context of defense strategy, observing that the same conduct may sustain a 206(2) claim even where intent for 206(1) cannot be established.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_005: Form ADV Disclosure Obligations for Whitmore's LP Interest Analyzed Beyond Restating Allegation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output repeatedly restates the allegation that Item 11 of WCA's Brochure failed to disclose Whitmore's 12% LP interest in Oceanic Fund I LP, and mentions Rule 206(4)-2 once in passing, but never identifies the specific Form ADV disclosure obligations applicable to this interest. The output does not analyze Item 11.A requiring disclosure of proprietary interests or conflicts, does not discuss Item 6's requirements regarding conflicts from principal investing alongside clients in a pooled vehicle with allocation discretion, and does not reference any SEC Staff guidance or Risk Alerts identifying undisclosed principal investments in pooled vehicles as a high-priority enforcement area. The output merely restates the allegation in various forms without identifying the specific disclosure requirement or regulatory reference that would make Whitmore's LP interest disclosable under Form ADV.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_006: Statute of Limitations Concern Under 28 U.S.C. Â§ 2462
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly raises the statute of limitations issue under 28 U.S.C. § 2462 in the 'Discrepancies and Defense Issues' section. It notes that SEC civil monetary penalties are subject to the five-year statute of limitations under 28 U.S.C. § 2462, and specifically addresses cherry-picking conduct dated to 2021, noting the five-year civil-penalty window closes around end of 2026. It also distinguishes between the penalty window and disgorgement exposure (referencing the NDAA 2021 ten-year window for scienter-based violations). The output further notes that the October 18, 2024 referral date establishes the operative date for tolling purposes and marks the formal transition triggering potential statute of limitations considerations for violations occurring during the January 1, 2021 – December 31, 2023 Review Period. While the output does not specifically cite Kokesh v. SEC, it does identify the core concern that early 2021 conduct may be approaching the five-year limitations period for civil penalties, satisfying the criterion's requirements.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_006: Specific 2021 Conduct Identified as Potentially Time-Barred
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output raises statute of limitations issues but does so in a generic, abstract manner. It notes that the cherry-picking scheme 'dates to 2021' and calculates that the five-year civil penalty window closes 'around the end of 2026,' but it does not specifically identify Q1 2021 cherry-picking conduct or early 2021 conduct as the allegations most at risk of being time-barred. The analysis applies the same boilerplate limitations language to multiple conduct categories (cherry-picking dated to 2021, cherry-picking dated to 2023, directed brokerage dated to various years) without singling out the Q1 2021 or early 2021 cherry-picking allegations as the specific subset facing the greatest limitations exposure. The criterion requires specific identification of which allegations are at risk, not merely a general observation that 2021 conduct exists within the review period.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_007: Date Discrepancy in Obstruction Allegation (April 15 vs. April 22)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not identify the specific date discrepancy between April 22, 2024 (the date cited in the referral notice for Whitmore's file deletion instruction) and April 15, 2024 (the date on the actual email exhibit whitmore-delgado-email.eml). The agent mentions April 22, 2024 as the deletion date throughout the document and references the email exhibit (Exhibit A, filename: whitmore-delgado-email.eml), but never flags the one-week discrepancy between the April 22 date stated in the referral narrative and the April 15 date shown on the email itself. The discrepancies section discusses other inconsistencies (file deletion year references to 2021 vs. early 2022, compensation figures, compliance manual deadline conflicts) but does not capture this specific April 15 vs. April 22 date conflict.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_007: Defense Significance of Date Discrepancy Discussed
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses the file deletion instruction and notes a discrepancy in the years mentioned (2021, early 2022, January 2021, March 2022) versus the SEC examination commencement date of March 11, 2024. The agent also discusses the 'steelman innocent reading' of the deletion instruction (routine IT cleanup vs. obstruction). The criterion specifically requires discussion of the April 15 vs. April 22 date discrepancy and its defense implications, which is absent from the output.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_008: Chao's Undisclosed Brokerage Account as Separate Violation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses Chao's personal brokerage account (ending -7823) receiving omnibus allocations and notes that Item 11 of the Brochure failed to disclose this account, but it does not identify the specific finding that this account does not appear on WCA's employee personal securities transaction report log (wca-employee-trading-log.xlsx), which lists only a Hartleigh IRA (ending -4401) for Chao. The agent never references the employee trading log or the discrepancy between what appears on that log versus Chao's actual accounts. The criterion requires identifying this omission from the trading log as a separate, distinct compliance failure under Rule 204A-1 (Code of Ethics requiring disclosure of personal brokerage accounts), independent of the cherry-picking allegation. The agent treats the account issue only in the context of the Form ADV disclosure failure and the cherry-picking scheme, not as a standalone Rule 204A-1 violation arising from failure to disclose the account to the firm itself via the required reporting mechanism.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_008: Rule 204A-1 (Code of Ethics) Referenced
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly references Rule 204A-1 multiple times and connects it to the failure to disclose Chao's personal brokerage account. For example, the output states: 'Rule 204A-1 under the Advisers Act [17 C.F.R. § 275.204A-1] --- Requires every investment adviser registered with the Commission to establish, maintain, and enforce a written code of ethics that includes, among other things, requirements for the reporting of personal securities transactions and holdings by supervised persons. To the extent that WCA's Code of Ethics and personal trading policies failed to capture and monitor the allocation of omnibus fills to Chao's personal brokerage account, or failed to require adequate reporting of personal securities transactions arising from such allocations, this rule is implicated.' The output also separately identifies this as a distinct regulatory violation from the cherry-picking scheme, noting it under 'Allegation Category 3' and stating 'Rule 204A-1 (17 C.F.R. § 275.204A-1) mandates written codes of ethics with personal trading reporting requirements, establishing the regulatory baseline against which Chao's undisclosed account allocations constitute violations.' This clearly satisfies the criterion.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_009: Omnibus Trade Percentage Discrepancy (73% vs. 78%)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output consistently cites the 73% figure (5,776/7,912 omnibus trades) throughout the document but never identifies or mentions any discrepancy between this figure and an 'approximately 78%' figure from the statistical exhibit (sec-quantitative-analysis-summary.xlsx). The discrepancies section lists three identified inconsistencies (file deletion year, undisclosed compensation total, and Access Person initial holdings report deadline), but the 73% vs. 78% omnibus trade percentage discrepancy is entirely absent from the output.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_009: Explanation of Why Omnibus Percentage Discrepancy Matters
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output does not mention any 73% vs. 78% discrepancy in omnibus trading percentage. The output consistently references 73% (5,776 of 7,912 trades) throughout without identifying any conflicting 78% figure or discussing implications of such a discrepancy. Since the criterion requires at least one concrete explanation of why a 73% vs. 78% discrepancy matters, and the agent never identifies or discusses this discrepancy at all, the criterion is not satisfied.

criteria_results.title: ISSUE_010: WCA's Respondeat Superior / Failure to Supervise Liability
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output names WCA as a respondent in all six allegation categories and notes that 'WCA bears primary responsibility for compliance with the Advisers Act and the rules promulgated thereunder, including responsibility for the conduct of its associated persons acting within the scope of their employment.' However, this is a generic respondeat superior statement applied to all conduct broadly, not a specific analysis of WCA's entity-level liability for Bellini's directed brokerage conduct. The output does not flag failure-to-supervise exposure under Section 203(e)(6) of the Investment Advisers Act, does not analyze how WCA's failure to detect the Lakeshore Trading arrangement through its compliance and best execution review processes supports a failure-to-supervise charge, and does not specifically connect WCA's supervisory failures (e.g., best execution reviews not flagging Lakeshore's higher commissions, compliance personnel not being informed of Bellini's relationship with Ostrowski) to entity-level liability for Bellini's specific conduct. The criterion requires more than merely naming WCA as a respondent; it requires flagging the specific respondeat superior/failure-to-supervise theory tied to Bellini's directed brokerage conduct and the compliance/best execution review failures that enabled it.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Cherry-Picking Allegation (12% LP Interest)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly identifies Whitmore's 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP, valued at approximately $22.2 million as of December 31, 2023, and directly connects this financial interest to his personal exposure in the cherry-picking allegation. The output states that 'the favorable allocation to Oceanic Fund I LP was motivated by Whitmore's personal financial interest in that vehicle' and describes how superior performance in Oceanic Fund I LP directly increases the value of his LP interest. The output also notes his dual financial incentives through both the LP interest and his indirect receipt of management fees and performance allocations via his 62% membership in WCA. This is addressed multiple times throughout the document under sections on the cherry-picking scheme, parties and ownership interests, and individuals at risk.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Form ADV Misrepresentation
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly addresses Whitmore's personal exposure to the Form ADV misrepresentation allegation in multiple places. It identifies that Item 11 of WCA's Brochure failed to disclose Whitmore's 12% limited partnership interest in Oceanic Fund I LP (valued at approximately $22.2 million), constituting a material omission of a direct financial conflict of interest. The output states that 'The Division alleges that Whitmore, as CEO, majority owner, and the individual with ultimate responsibility for the Firm's regulatory filings...were responsible for ensuring the accuracy and completeness of the Firm's Form ADV disclosures.' It also notes violations of Section 207 of the Advisers Act and Rule 206(4)-1(a)(5) in connection with the misleading Form ADV disclosures, and explicitly lists Whitmore as implicated in 'Allegation Category 2 (Misleading Form ADV Disclosures).' The output further notes that no interim amendments were filed to correct these omissions despite Rule 204-1(a) obligations, establishing Whitmore's ongoing personal exposure.

criteria_results.title: Whitmore's Exposure: Obstruction Allegation (File Deletion)
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output extensively addresses Whitmore's personal exposure to the obstruction allegation. It identifies that Whitmore instructed IT Administrator Tomás Delgado to delete trade allocation spreadsheets from the shared network drive on April 22, 2024, during the pendency of the SEC examination. The output notes this occurred after document requests had been served, discusses the inference of obstructive intent, cites Section 209(e) of the Advisers Act as the statutory basis, and even flags parallel criminal exposure under 18 U.S.C. § 1519 and § 1505. Whitmore is explicitly named as the individual who gave the deletion instruction and is identified as implicated in the obstruction allegation category.

criteria_results.title: Individuals at Risk: Chao's Personal Exposure Summarized
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly addresses Chao's personal exposure in multiple sections. It identifies her 81.6% profitable allocation rate (71 of 87 omnibus allocations were same-day profitable), the ~$438,000 in excess profits in her personal account broken down by year ($162K in 2021, $189K in 2022, $87K in 2023), her undisclosed personal brokerage account (Account No. ending -7823) at Meridian Clearing Corp. receiving allocations from the firm's omnibus account, and her implication in the cherry-picking allegation as Head of Trading who exercised day-to-day discretion over allocation decisions. The output also notes her potential individual sanctions including industry bar, civil monetary penalties, disgorgement, and injunctive relief.

criteria_results.title: Individuals at Risk: Bellini's Personal Exposure Summarized
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output thoroughly addresses Bellini's personal exposure. It identifies his relationship with Ostrowski (college roommates at University of Virginia 1999-2003, Ostrowski holds 40% of Lakeshore Trading), the excess commissions of $291,400 (4.2 cents vs 1.1 cents per share on 23 directed trades totaling $48.7M notional), and the undisclosed personal compensation of $92,600 ($45,000 initiation fee + $47,600 in monthly dues over 17 months). criteria_results.title: Total Alleged Ill-Gotten Gains / Client Harm Stated
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly states the total alleged ill-gotten gains/client harm as $8,622,000, broken down as: cherry-picking excess profits ($8,238,000 = $7,800,000 to Oceanic Fund I LP + $438,000 to Chao's personal account) + excess commissions paid to Lakeshore Trading ($291,400) + undisclosed compensation received by Bellini from Ostrowski ($92,600 = $45,000 initiation fee + $47,600 in monthly dues). criteria_results.title: Hargrove & Tilton Review: Failure to Test Trade Allocation Noted
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly mentions multiple times that the Hargrove & Tilton annual compliance review (dated December 8, 2023) did not test trade allocation patterns despite the firm's heavy reliance on omnibus trading.
